items.q: What is the difference between a four-figure and six-figure grid reference?
items.correct: Four-figure references identify a 1km square (eastings then northings); six-figure references pinpoint a location within that square to 100m accuracy by adding tenths.
items.misconception: Six-figure references are just longer versions of the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Four-figure references are for OS maps and six-figure references are for atlases.
items.why: Accuracy differs by an order of magnitude. Students forget the 'along the corridor and up the stairs' order (eastings before northings) — a common exam error costing easy marks.

items.q: What is hydraulic action in a river?
items.correct: The force of moving water itself compressing air into cracks in the river bank or bed, weakening rock until pieces break off — no sediment needed.
items.misconception: Hydraulic action is when rocks in the river smash into each other.
items.random_wrong: Hydraulic action is when the river dissolves rocks chemically.
items.why: Students mix up the four river erosion processes. Hydraulic action = water force; abrasion = water using rocks to scrape; attrition = rocks hitting rocks; solution (corrosion) = chemical dissolving.

items.q: How are fold mountains like the Himalayas formed?
items.correct: By the collision of two continental plates (India and Eurasia) where neither can subduct, so the crust crumples and is forced upwards over millions of years.
items.misconception: Fold mountains form when one plate slides under another into the mantle.
items.random_wrong: Fold mountains form from giant volcanoes erupting upwards.
items.why: Students confuse continent-continent collision with subduction. Continental crust is too buoyant to subduct, so it crumples — distinct from oceanic-continental boundaries like the Andes.

items.q: Which test compares observed vs expected frequencies?
items.why: Correct answer: Chi-squared. “Spearman's” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Alonso. “Burgess” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Common significance level for hypothesis testing?
items.why: The real figure is 0.05 p. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
